fact: September 17 sits exactly 4 days before the autumn equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal twins!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and cozy winter hideouts in your home!
Maple trees in cold regions are just starting their color-changing magic show, with sugar rushing from leaves back to roots.
Why do sunsets look extra orange around now? Earth's tilting angle creates thicker atmosphere for sunlight to travel through!
September 17 marks day 260 of the year - that means only 105 days left until New Year's Eve!
Monarch butterflies in North America are in full migration mode, flying up to 100 miles per day toward Mexico!
This date falls smack in the middle of harvest season, when pumpkins are rapidly gaining weight - some grow 40 pounds in a single day!
Squirrels enter their hyperactive hoarding phase around this time, burying up to 10,000 nuts before winter hits!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, September 17 brings the sweet smell of spring flowers blooming everywhere!
Morning dew becomes super heavy around this date in many places because warm days meet surprisingly chilly nights!
Geese flying south create their famous V-formations right around now - the lead goose works 20% harder than the others!
